Facts.text: "The Diamond Sutra", the world's oldest surviving and dated printed book is printed in Chinese and made into a scroll
Edgar, Anglo-Saxon King of England and his consort Aelfthryth are crowned at Bath on Whit Sunday -  possibly a second crowning as already ruled for 14 years
Elfrida, consort of King Edgar is crowned Queen of England at Bath Abbey, first coronation of a Saxon Queen
Fifty-four members of the Knights Templar are burned at the stake in France for being heretics
Jews are expelled from Styria Austria
Peasants besiege Frankenburg estate in Upper Austria
Peter Stuyvesant arrives in New Amsterdam
Netherlands & Cologne sign peace treaty
French admiral Jean d'Estrees' naval fleet runs aground on Aves-islands, Curacao, ends French control and ushers in an age of Piracy in the Caribbean
Battle of Bantry Bay, French & English naval battle
English troops of William Phips conquer Port Royal, Nova Scotia
Battle of Fontenoy (Doornik): Austrian Succession war
British parliament accepts Consolidation Act, to reorganise the Royal Navy
1st US fire insurance policy issued, in Philadelphia
Amsterdam Theater destroyed by fire, 18 killed, in Amsterdam, Northern Netherlands
Britain & Tippu Sahib of Mysore sign peace treaty
Columbia River discovered & named by US Captain Robert Gray
The Waltz is introduced into English ballrooms; some observers consider it disgusting and immoral

In Australia, William Lawson, Gregory Blaxland and William Wentworth, lead an expedition westwards from Sydney. Their route opens up inland Australia for continued expansion throughout the 19th century.
American Bible Society forms (NY)
"Lady-of-the-Lake" strikes iceberg & sinks in North Atlantic; kills 215
Work starts on 1st brick building in San Francisco
Minnesota admitted as 32nd US state
Confederates scuttle CSS Virginia off Norfolk, Virginia
Confederate Brigadier General Meriwether Jeff Thompson surrenders at Jacksonport, Arkansas
Treaty of London drawn, granting Luxembourg full independence and neutrality
George "Charmer" Zettlein pitches the 1st 9 inning shutout
American RR Union strikes Pullman Sleeping Car Co
Washington Senator catcher Charlie Farrell throws out 8 attempted stealers
A derailment outside Lompoc, California, kills 32 Shriners when their chartered train jumps off the tracks at a switch near Surf Depot
Bank of San Francisco incorporated
Montana's Glacier National Park forms
The United States becomes a signatory to the Buenos Aires copyright treaty
Cincinnati Reds pitcher Hod Eller no-hits St Louis Cardinals, 6-0 at Redland Field, Cincinnati
Tel Aviv is 1st all Jewish municipality
The Allied Supreme Council warns Germany to pay reparations or the entire Ruhr Valley will be occupied; Germany agrees
10 HRs hit in Phillies 20-14 victory over St Louis Cardinals at the Baker Bowl in Philadelphia
Cartel des Gauches wins French parliamentary election
Communist Party of Holland splits
Kara-Kalpak Autonomous Region constituted in RSFSR
Airship Norge leaves Ny-Ålesund, on the Norwegian island of Spitsbergen, for 1st air crossing of Arctic Ocean
England's football team beat Belgium 9-1
Louis B Mayer forms Academy of Motion Picture Arts & Sciences
General Electric opens 1st TV-station in Schenectady, New York
1st regularly scheduled TV broadcasts (3 nights per week)
Dr Annie Webb Blanton forms Delta Kappa Gamma Society in Austin, Texas
"M" Fritz Lang's first sound film starring Peter Lorre premieres in Berlin
Credit-Anstalt, Austria's largest bank, fails, beginning financial collapse of Central Europe
Great dust bowl storm sweeps across the American and Canadian prairies traveling as far as the East Coast
Los Glaciares National Park established in Patagonia, Argentina, the country's largest national park (UNESCO World Heritage Site 1981)
NY World's Fair reopens
1st German Messerschmidt 109F shot down above England
Japanese troops conquer Kalewa

WWII: US Army 7th Division makes amphibious landing on US territory of Attu, Aleutian, which had been occupied by Japanese Imperial forces since June; US re-takes the island after two weeks of fierce battle
Opposition group surprise attack post office Washer
US Marines conquer Awatsha Draw, Okinawa
1st night game at Boston Braves Field (Giants 5, Braves 1)
United Malays National Organisation is created.
BF Goodrich announced the development of tubeless tire (Akron Ohio)
Laos accepts constitution for parliamentary democracy
Haganah takes control of Safed & port of Haifa
Luigi Einaudi elected President of Italy
By a vote of 37-12, Israel becomes 59th member of UN
Siam renames itself Thailand
Belgium mine disaster at Borinage, 39 die
American engineer Jay Forrester applies for patent for computer core memory
Tornado kills 114 in Waco Texas ($39M damage)
Israel attacks Gaza
Pinky Lee Show last airs on NBC-TV
Gabriel Paris forms government of Colombia
US performs atmospheric nuclear test at Bikini Island
US performs its second atmospheric nuclear test of the day, this one at Enewetak Atoll in the Marshall Islands
"Kookie, Kookie, Lend Me Your Comb" by Edd Byrnes & Connie Stevens hits #4
French liner "France" launched
US performs atmospheric nuclear test at Christmas Island
US sends troops to Thailand
"Puff (The Magic Dragon)" single by Peter, Paul & Mary hits #2; Peter Yarrow adapted a poem that college classmate Lenny Lipton had left behind after borrowing Yarrow's typewriter
Racial bomb attacks in Birmingham Alabama
1st of 2 cyclones in less than a month kills 35,000 (India)
Bangladesh windstorm kills 17,000
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
West Indies becomes 1st holders of the Frank Worrell Cricket Trophy
100,000,000th US phone connected
Great Britain, Ireland & Denmark apply for membership of the EEC
Stanley Cup Final, Montreal Forum, Montreal, Quebec: Defenceman J. C. Tremblay fires home the winner as Montreal Canadiens edge St Louis Blues, 3-2 for a 4-0 series sweep
Students & police battle in Paris, 100s injured
Toronto Transit Commission opens the largest expansion of its Bloor-Danforth Line, going to Scarborough in the east, Etobicoke in the west
"The Long and Winding Road" becomes Beatles' last American single release
Henry Marrow is murdered in a violent racially-motivated crime in Oxford, North Carolina.
Cleveland's pitcher Steve Dunning hits a grand slam off Oakland Athletics pitcher Diego Seguí
Stanley Cup Final, Madison Square Garden, NYC: Wayne Cashman scores twice as Boston Bruins beat NY Rangers, 3-0 to take title, 4-2
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Citing government misconduct, Daniel Ellsberg has his charges for his involvement in releasing the "Pentagon Papers" to The New York Times dismissed
Dutch government of Uyl forms
"Tubular Bells" by Mike Oldfield hits #7
Israel signs an agreement with European Economic Market
Last broadcast of "Marcus Welby, MD" on ABC-TV
Margaret A Brewer is 1st female general in the US Marine Corps
Comet C/1983 H1 (IRAS-Araki-Alcock) approaches 0.0312 AUs of Earth
Johan Cruijff quits soccer
Tigers set best 30 game start record (26-4)
Transit of Earth as seen on Mars
56 die and at least 265 are injured at Bradford City football ground in the worst fire in English football history
Booby trap bomb kills 86 people in India
Dave Concepcion becomes 4th Cincinnati Red teammate to get 2,000 hits, others includePete Rose, Tony Perez and César Cedeño
First heart-lung transplant takes place in Baltimore
France performs nuclear test
France performs nuclear test at Mururoa Atoll
Kenya announces worldwide ban on ivory to preserve its elephant herds
Paramaribo Suriname TV studio destroyed by fire
"Naked Gun 33 1/3: The Final Insult" released in France
6 white racists sentenced to death in South Africa
More than 170 countries agree to extend the Nuclear Nonproliferation Treaty indefinitely and without conditions to prevent the spread of nuclear weapons
Florida Marlin Al Leiter no hits Colorado Rockies, 11-0
Valujet DC-9 crashes in Miami, 109 die
NY Mets C Everett & Butch Huskey are 9th to hit consecutive pinch HRs
India conducts three underground nuclear tests in Pokhran, including a thermonuclear device
India's population officially reaches 1 billion - Astha Arora named India's billionth baby
Princess Margriet of the Netherlands unveils the Man With Two Hats monument in Ottawa and Apeldoorn, symbolically linking both the Netherlands and Canada for their assistance throughout World War II
An American soldier in Iraq opened fire on a counseling center at Camp Liberty in Bagdhad, leaving 5 other US soldiers dead and 3 soldiers wounded
Chinese scientists break world record by transferring photons over 97 kilometers using quantum teleportation
43 people are killed in two car bombings in Reyhanlı, Turkey
Barcelona wins its fourth La Liga title in four years
Thousands protest against the construction of a waste incineration plant in Hangzhou, China
Mass murder-suicide in Margaret River, Australia, grandfather shoots six members of his family and himself
Most popular names in the US for 2017 revealed as Emma for girls, Liam for boys
Carbon dioxide levels in the earth's atmosphere hit levels not seen for 3 million years at 415 parts per million, according to the Mauna Loa Observatory, Hawaii
Militants attack Zaver Pearl-Continental Hotel in Gwadar, Pakistan killing five, Balochistan Liberation Army claim responsibility
Colombian airline Avianca, and world's 2nd oldest, becomes 1st major airline to file for bankruptcy due to COVID-19
World Health Organization warns “extreme vigilance” needed as many countries begin easing lockdowns
China census figures show country's population 1.41 billion people, growth at its slowest since 1960s, (+0.53 down from +0.57)
Northern Ireland inquest into 1971 Ballymurphy killing of ten people finds people were innocent and killed (nine by soldiers) using unjustified force
Al Jazeera journalist Shireen Abu Akleh shot and killed by Israeli forces in city of Jenin, in the West Bank
Costa Rica declares a state of emergency after a ransomware attack infiltrates and cripples several government agencies
First ever US government report into Indian boarding school deaths released (not complete), documents more than 500 deaths across 400 schools and 50 gravesites over 150 years
Rare strong solar storm reaches Earth prompting the US National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration to issue a geomagnetic storm warning
Newly built city of Constantinople (Byzantium) dedicated to EmperorConstantine the Great, becomes the capital of the Eastern Roman Empire
Matilda of Flanders, wife ofWilliam the Conqueror, is crowned Queen of England in Westminster Abbey, London
Holy Roman EmperorFrederick IBarbarossa and 100,000 crusaders depart Regensburg for the Third Crusade
Earlier known performance ofShakespeare's comedy "The Winter's Tale" at the Globe theatre, London, recorded by Simon Forman
Pennsylvania Hospital founded by Dr. Thomas Bond andBenjamin Franklin, in Philadelphia
French naturalistJean-Baptiste Lamarckgives his first lecture outlining his theories of evolution at the Muséum national d'histoire naturelle in Paris, France
Launch of HMS Beagle, the ship that would later take a youngCharles Darwinon his famous scientific voyage
AmericanCharles Wilkeslands at Fort Nisqually in Puget Sound
US Civil War: Battle of Yellow Tavern in Henrico County, Virginia; Union Army prevails and Confederate GeneralJ.E.B. Stuartis mortally wounded
BritishQueen Victoriaissues charter granting city status to the Borough of Liverpool
Bedřich Smetana's opera "Libuše" premieres at the National Theate, in Prague, Kingdom of Bohemia
The Otsu Scandal: While visiting Japan, Prince Nicholas (later TsarNicholas II) survives an assassination attempt
Henri Desgrangeestablishes world's 1st bicycle world record, travelling 35.325 km (21.95 miles) an hour
James J. JeffriesKOsJames J Corbettin 23 for heavyweight boxing title
Andrew Carnegiedonates $1.5m to build the Peace Palace in The Hague, Netherlands, an international law administrative building and home for the Permanent Court of Arbitration
KingGeorge Vgrants Royal Letters Patent to New Zealand establishing office of Governor-General as Monarch's representative in the country
Washington Senators future Baseball Hall of Fame aceWalter Johnsonpitches 12 scoreless innings in a famous 0-0 duel with Jack Quinn of the NY Yankees at the Polo Grounds
Pulitzer Prize for poetry awarded toRobert Frostfor "New Hampshire: A Poem with Notes and Grace Notes"
Joseph Stalinorders Soviet troops to invade Manchuria, resulting in a 4-month battle that inflicted heavy losses on Japan
William Faulkner's collections of short stories, "Go Down, Moses", is published
German  Luftwaffe armouredHermann Göringdivision in Tunisia surrenders - its commander  Colonel Joseph Schmid escapes
Eugene Ionesco's first play "La Cantatrice Chauve" (The Bald Soprano) premieres in Paris
Winston Churchillcriticizes US Secretary of StateJohn Foster Dulles' domino theory
Elvis Presley's 1st entry on UK charts with "Heartbreak Hotel"
New York Yankees catcherYogi Berra's errorless streak of 148 games ends
Antonio Segnibecomes President of Italy
LA Dodgers pitcherSandy Koufaxthrows his second career no-hitter; beatsJuan Marichaland the SF Giants, 8-0
Irish actorRichard Harrisreleases single "MacArthur Park"; it becomes a million-seller topping the charts in Canada and Australia, and peaking at #2 in US and #4 in UK
British comedy troupeMonty Pythonforms, made up of Graham Chapman,John Cleese, Terry Gilliam, Eric Idle, Terry Jones, andMichael Palin
Giants tradeWillie Maysto Mets for pitcher Charlie Williams & cash
John Lennonsays his phone is tapped by FBI on "The Dick Cavett Show"
Ground-breaking Indian crime film "Zanjeer" directed by Prakash Mehra and starring "The Angry Young Man"Amitabh Bachchanreleased
ABC Records releasesSteely Dansingle "Rikki Don't Lose That Number" from the "Pretzel Logic "album; it peaks at #4 in the US, making it their biggest hit
Capitol Records releasesNatalie Cole's debut album "Inseparable"; it features two hits "This Will Be (An Everlasting Love)" and the title track, and wins 2 Grammy Awards
Ted Turnermanages an Atlanta Braves game
MLB Philadelphia PhilliesPete Rose, 39, steals second, third, & home in the same inning
Andrew Lloyd Webber's musical "Cats" (based on poetry byT. S. Eliot) directed by Trevor Nunn, opens at the New London Theatre in the West End, London; runs for 8,949 performances
Madonna's "Crazy For You" single goes #1
PopeJohn Paul IIarrival in Netherlands marred by violent protest
Trial of former Gestapo commandantKlaus Barbiebegins in France for war crimes during World War II
217th & final episode of TVsoap opera "Dynasty"is aired
US PresidentGeorge H. W. Bushorders 1,900 additional troops to Panama
NY Yankees tradeDave Winfieldto Angels for Mike Witt
Film adaptation of Mbongeni Ngema and Hugh Masekela's anti-apartheid musical "Sarafina!", starring Leleti Khumalo, Miriam Makeba, andWhoopi Goldberg, premieres at Cannes Film Festival in France
Chess-playing computer Deep Blue developed by IBM defeats Russian grandmasterGarry Kasparov3.5-2.5 in chess
PopeBenedict XVIcanonizes the first Brazilian-born saint, Frei Galvão
Spanish-American singerCharo(57?) makes a guest appearance on the Latin-themed VH1 reality show Viva Hollywood
Patrick Kanescores his first career hat-trick in the NHL playoffs against the Vancouver Canucks
David Cameronbecomes Prime Minister of the United Kingdom after forming a coalition government between the Liberal Democrats and his own Conservative Party
Imelda Marcoswins election to the House of Representatives of the Philippines, representing Ilocos Norte province
Bayern Munich set a new  Bundesliga points record of 91
Record price for a work of art at auction:Picasso's The Women of Algiers (Version ‘O’) sells for US$179.3 million at Christies in New York
Brazilian senate votes to suspend PresidentDilma Rousseffand put her on trial for budgetary violations
American actress and #MeToo activistAlyssa Milanourges women to go on a "sex strike" after Georgia passes a new abortion law
South African parliamentary elections return rulingCyril Ramaphosa's ANC party to power with reduced majority (58%)
First major US offshore windfarm off the coast of Massachusetts approved byBidenadministration

British Prime MinisterSpencer Percevalis assassinated by John Bellingham in the lobby of the House of Commons, London. Ironically, descendants of both later stand for the same seat in Parliament at the same time, but neither wins
Israeli soldiers captureAdolf Eichmannin Buenos Aires
Ellis Island added to Statue of Liberty National monument
Christian I, German Prince of Anhalt-Bernburg who led an army of 21,000 Bohemians at the Battle of White Mountain, born in Bernburg (d. 1630)
Niwa Nagashige, Japanese daimyō (feudal lord), serving the Oda clan, born in Japan (d. 1637)
Cornelis van Alkemade, Dutch historian, collector of antiquities and author (Coinage of the Counts of Holland), born in Noordwijk, Dutch Republic (d. 1737)[1]
Baron Münchhausen, German adventurer, soldier and storyteller (The Adventures of Baron Munchausen), born in Hanover (d. 1797)
Petrus Camper, Dutch anatomist and anthropologist who studied comparative anatomy, paleontology and the facial angle, born in Leiden, Netherlands (d. 1789)
Johann Gottfried Seyfert, German composer and violinist, born in Augsburg, Electorate of Bavaria, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1772)
Victoire of France, French princess, daughter of King Louis XV and Queen Marie Leszczyńska, born in Versailles, Kingdom of France (d. 1799)
Johann Friedrich Blumenbach, German anthropologist and naturalised credited as a founder of zoology and anthropology, born in Gotha, Saxe-Gotha-Altenburg (d. 1840)
Henri-Jean Rigel, French composer, music director, and "pianiste de la musique particulière de l’Empereur et Roi" to Napoleon, born in Paris, France (d. 1852)
Jan Václav Voříšek, Czech pianist, organist, and composer, born in Vamberk, Kingdom of Bohemia (d. 1825)
John Lowell, American philanthropist, born in Massachusetts (d. 1836)
Henri Labrouste, French architect (Sainte-Geneviève Library, reading room of the National Library of France), born in Paris, France (d. 1875)[1]
Chang & Eng Bunker, Chinese-Thai Siamese twins, born in Samutsongkram, Thailand (d. 1874)
(Francesca) "Fanny" Cerrito, Italian ballerina and choreographer (Rosida), born in Naples, Kingdom of the Two Sicilies (d. 1909)
[Carlos] Charles John Stolbrand, Swedish-American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Skåne, Sweden (d. 1894)
Alfred Stevens, Belgian painter, born in Brussels, Belgium (d. 1906)
Jean-Léon Gérôme, French painter and sculptor, born in Rome, Italy (d. 1904)
Jean-Baptiste Carpeaux, French sculptor and painter, born in Valenciennes, France (d. 1875)
Septimus Winner, American songwriter (Listen To The Mockingbird), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1902)
John Converse Starkweather, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Cooperstown, New York (d. 1890)
Kārlis Baumanis, Latvian composer (Dievs, svētī Latviju! (“God bless Latvia!”), the national anthem of Latvia), born in Viļķene Parish, Courland Governorate, Russian Empire (d. 1905)
Walter Goodman, English painter, illustrator and author, born in London (d. 1912)
Filippo Capocci, Italian organ virtuoso and composer, born in Rome, Papal States (d. 1911)
Ernst von Possart, German actor and theatre director (Bayerische Hoftheater), born in Berlin, Germany (d. 1921)
Charles W. Fairbanks, 26th Vice President of the United States (Republican: 1905-09) and Senator, born in Unionville Center, Ohio (d. 1918)
Jack Blackham, Australian cricket wicketkeeper (35 Tests, 4 x 50s, 61 dismissals; Victoria CA), born in Melbourne, Australia (d. 1932)
Ottmar Mergenthaler, German-American inventor who invented the linotype machine that revolutionized printing, born in Hachtel, Kingdom of Württemberg (d. 1899)
Carl Hauptmann, German writer, born in Obersalzbrunn (d. 1921)

Frederick Russell Burnham, American scout and adventurer whose friendship with Baden-Powell inspired the founding of the international scouting movement, born in Tivoli, a Dakota Sioux Indian reservation in modern-day Minnesota, (d. 1947)
Francisco Lacerda, Portuguese composer, conductor, and musicologist, born in Ribeira Seca, Portugal (d. 1934)
Frank Schlesinger, American astronomer and director of the Yale University Observatory (1920-41), born in New York City (d. 1943)
Jan Machiel Reyskens, oldest Belgian-Dutch male to live, born in Limburg, Belgium (d. 1990 aged 111)
Jan van Gilse, Dutch composer (Frau Helga von Staveren; Thijl), conductor, and anti-Nazi resistance fighter, born in Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 1944)
Theodore von Kármán, Hungarian-born American physicist and aeronautic engineer (co-founder NASA Jet Propulsion Laboratory), born in Budapest, Austria-Hungary (d. 1963)[1]
Joseph Marx, Austrian composer and music critic, born in Graz, Austria (d. 1964)
Paul Wittgenstein, Austrian-American concert pianist who adapted to left-hand only after losing right arm in WWI, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1961)[1]
Willis Augustus Lee, American World War II admiral (Guadalcanal) and sport shooter (5 Olympic golds 1920), born in Natlee, Kentucky (d. 1945)
Henry Morgenthau Jr., US Secretary of Treasury (1934-45) who helped design the New Deal and Lend Lease programs, born in New York City (d. 1967)
Margaret Rutherford, English actress (Murder Most Foul, The V.I.P.s), born in London (d. 1972)
Anton Mussert, Dutch fascist (co-founded the National Socialist Movement in the Netherlands), born in Werkendam, Netherlands (d. 1946)
Martha Graham, American dancer and choreographer (Appalachian Spring), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 1991)
Jan Parandowski, Polish writer and translator, born in Lwów, Austria-Hungary (now Lviv, Ukraine) (d. 1978)
Jiddu Krishnamurti, Indian philosopher, writer (Kingdom of Happiness), and poet (Song of Life), born in Madanapalle, British (d. 1986)
William Grant Still, American composer ("Afro-American" Symphony), born in Woodville, Mississippi (d. 1978)
Josip Slavenski, Croatian composer and pedagogue, born in Čakovec, Croatia-Slavonia, Austria-Hungary (d. 1955)
Philippo de Pisis, Italian painter and poet, born in Ferarra, Itay (d. 1956)
Boerneef [pen name for Izak Wilhelmus van der Merwe], South African writer (Tweetalige Woordeboek), born in Ceres, Cape Colony (d. 1967)
Robert E. Gross, American aviation businessman, born in Newton, Massachusetts (d. 1961)
Paulino Masip, Spanish playwright, screenwriter and novelist (El Diario de Hamlet Garcia), born in La Granadella (d. 1963)
Mari Sandoz, American author (Cheyenne Autumn), born in Hay Springs, Nebraska (d. 1966)

Rose Ausländer (née Scherzer), Jewish German-American poet (Der Regenbogen (The Rainbow); Denn wo ist Heimat? (Then Where is the Homeland)), and newspaper editor, born in Czernowitz, Duchy of Bukovina, Austria-Hungary (d. 1988)[1]
Bidu Sayão, Brazilian opera soprano (NY Metropolitan Opera: 1937-52) and celebrated musician, born in Itaguaí, Rio de Janeiro, Brazil (d. 1999)
Kaarlo Sarkia, Finnish poet (Unen Kaivo), born in Kiikka, Grand Duchy of Finland (d. 1945)
Charlie Gehringer, American Baseball HOF 2nd baseman (6 x MLB All Star; World Series 1935; AL MVP & batting champion 1937; Detroit Tigers), born in Fowlerville, Michigan (d. 1993)
Guus Weitzel, Dutch radio personality and actor, born in Teterings, Netherlands (d. 1989)
Marjorie Sykes, British educator, peace activist, and Indian independence campaigner, born in Mexborough, Yorkshire, England (d. 1995)
Jacqueline Cochran, US pilot and 1st woman to break the sound barrier, born in Muscogee, Florida (d. 1980)
Kent Taylor [Louis Weiss], American actor (Boston Blackie, Rough Riders), born in Nashua, Iowa (d. 1987)
Rip Sewell, American baseball pitcher (MLB All-Star 1943–46; NL wins leader 1943; Pittsburgh Pirates 1938–49), born in Decatur, Alabama (d. 1989)
Herbert Murrill, British organist, composer (The Song of the Birds; Carillon), and educator (Royal Academy of Music, 1933-52), born in London, England (d. 1952)
René Bousquet, French Vichy-police chief (deported Jews), born in Montauban, Tarn-et-Garonne (d. 1993)
Jorge Croner de Vasconcelos, Portuguese composer, born in Lisbon, Portugal (d. 1974)
Edgar Beck, English civil engineer (led firm Mowlem that reconstructed Downing St, built new London Bridge and NatWest Tower), born in London England (d. 2000)
Mitchell Sharp, Canadian politician and Liberal Cabinet minister, born in Winnipeg, Manitoba (d. 2004)
Doodles Weaver [Winstead Sheffield Weaver], American comedian (Spike Jones & City Slickers, The Zodiac Killer), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 1983)
Foster Brooks, American comedian and actor (The Villain, Oddballs, Mork & Mindy), born in Louisville, Kentucky (d. 2001)
Clare Grundman, American composer, born in Cleveland, Ohio (d. 1996)
Robert Jungk, Austrian writer, journalist and peace campaigner (The Nuclear State), born in Berlin (d. 1994)
[Salvador] Tutti Camarata, American composer and producer (Vic Damone Show), born in New Jersey (d. 2005)
Haroun Tazieff, French-Belgian volcanologist, author and cinematographer of volcanic eruptions (The Violent Earth), born in Warsaw, Congress Poland (d. 1998)[1]
Camilo Jose Cela, Spanish writer (Nobel Prize 1989), born in Padrón, La Coruña (d. 2002)
Montague Woodhouse, British military officer and politician (organized the resistance forces in Crete WWII, MI6 agent), born in England (d. 2001)
Richard Feynman, American theoretical physicist, born in Queens, New York (d. 1988)
Beryl Bryden, British jazz and blues singer, and washboard player, born in Norwich, Norfolk, England (d. 1998)
Denver Pyle, American actor (The Dukes of Hazzard, The Andy Griffith Show), born in Cincinnati, Ohio (d. 1997)
Hildegard Hamm-Brücher, German politician, born in Essen, Germany (d. 2016)
Ian Percival, British politician (Conservative Party) and Solicitor General, born in England (d. 1998)
Ameurfina Melencio-Herrera, Filipino Supreme Court jurist, born in Cabanatuan, Philippines (d. 2020)
Nestor Chylak, American Baseball HOF umpire (AL 1954-78; 3 x ALCS; 5 x World Series; 6 x MLB All-Star games), born in Olyphant, Pennsylvania (d. 1992)
Gordon White, British business tycoon and co-founder of corporate raider Hanson Group, born in Hull, England (d. 1995)
Antony Hewish, English radio astronomer (led team that discovered pulsars, Nobel Prize for Physics 1974), born in Fowey, England (d. 2021)[1]
Eugene Dynkin, Russian mathematician(The Dynkin diagram), born in Leningrad, Soviet Union (d. 2014)
Jackie Milburn, English soccer striker (13 caps; Newcastle United 353 games), born in Ashington, England (d. 1988)
Tim Flock, American auto racer (Winston Cup 1952, 55), born in Fort Payne, Alabama (d. 1998)
Edward J. King, American politician, 66th Governor of Massachusetts (1979-83), born in Chelsea, Massachusetts (d. 2006)
Rhodes Boyson, British politician and author (Black Papers), born in Haslingden, Lancashire (d. 2012)
William Glasser, psychiatrist (reality therapy, choice theory), born in Cleveland, Ohio (d. 2013)
Ernest Harrison, British electronics and telecommunications executive (CEO of Racal Electronics, 1966-2000; Vodafone, 1981-2000}, born in Hackney, London, England (d. 2009)
Frank Thring, Australian actor (El Cid, Ben-Hur), born in Melbourne, Victoria (d. 1994)
Yvonne Furneaux [Scatcherd], British actress (La Dolce Vita; Repulsion), born in Roubaix, France (d. 2024)
Bernard Fox, Welsh actor (Bewitched, Titanic, Hogan's Heroes), born in Port Talbot, Wales (d. 2016)
Gene Savoy, American author, explorer and religious leader, Head Bishop of the International Community of Christ (1971-2007), born in Bellingham, Washington (d. 2007)
Mort Sahl, American stand-up comedian, political satirist, writer, and TV personality (The Big Party), born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 2021)
Sir Anthony Hollis, British High Court Judge, Family Division (1982-97), born in England (d. 2003)
Zilpha Keatly Snyder, American Newbury Award-winning children's literature author (Headless Cupid; The Egypt Game), born in Lemoore, California (d. 2014)
Brother Andrew [Andrew van der Bijl], Dutch Christian missionary (Open Doors), born in Alkmaar, Netherlands (d. 2022)
Yaacov Agam, Israeli sculptor, born in Rishon LeZion, Mandate Palestine
Edward Anson, Australian British Royal Navy pilot (Korean War), officer (Vice Admiral), and ship captain (HMS Ark Royal), born in Adelaide, Australia (d. 2014)
Edsger Dijkstra, Dutch computer scientist (Dijkstra's algorithm), born in Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 2002)[1]
Edward Kamau Brathwaite, Caribbean poet (Born to Slow Horses) and academic, born in Bridgetown, Barbados (d. 2020)
Gordon Langford [Colman], British composer and arranger of brass, choral, and orchestral music (Facets of Glass; Rhapsody for Trombone), born in Edgware, England (d. 2017)
James "Bud" Ekins, American film stuntman (The Great Escape; Bullitt), born in Hollywood, Los Angeles, California (d. 2007)
Stanley Elkin, American novelist (George Mills; The MacGuffin; The Living End), satirist, and college professor (Washington University, 1960-95), born in Brooklyn, New York City (d. 1995)
William Carey, American businessman and philanthropist (W.P. Carey & Co. LLC), born in Baltimore, Maryland (d. 2012)
Francisco Umbral, Spanish novelist, journalist and essayist, born in Madrid, Spain (d. 2007)
Henriette Santer, Dutch-English clinical psychologist, born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1994)
Valentino [Garavani], Italian fashion designer and founder of the Valentino company, born in Milan, Italy
Carlos Lyra, Brazilian bossa nova guitarist and singer-songwriter, born in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil (d. 2023)[1]
Herbert Migdoll, American artist and photographer best known for his work with the Joffrey Ballet, born in Jersey City, New Jersey (d. 2025)[1]
Jim. Jeffords, American politician (Senator-R+I-Vermont 1989-2007, Rep-R-VT, 1975-89), born in Rutland, Vermont (d. 2014)
Christopher "Kit" Lambert, British record producer and manager of The Who, born in Knightsbridge, London (d. 1981)
Doug McClure, American actor (Checkmate, Virginian, Roots), born in Glendale, California (d. 1995)
Carla Bley [Lovella Borg], American jazz pianist, organist, bandleader, and composer (Escalator Over The Hill; Fictitious Sports), born in Oakland, California (d. 2023)[1]
